questions.question: Which statement about space complexity is correct?
options: Space is measured on the input tape
Space is measured on the work tape (excluding input)
L = P always
NL is a subset of L
questions.explanation: Space complexity counts work-tape cells used beyond the input. The input tape is read-only and not counted. L ⊆ NL ⊆ P ⊆ PSPACE — but whether L = NL or NL = L is unknown.

questions.question: What does Savitch's theorem state?
options: P = PSPACE
NSPACE(f(n)) ⊆ SPACE(f(n)²)
NL = L
Reachability is in P
questions.explanation: Savitch's theorem (1970): any problem solvable in nondeterministic space f(n) can be solved in deterministic space f(n)². The proof uses divide-and-conquer reachability. For f(n) = log n, this gives NL ⊆ SPACE((log n)²).

questions.question: Savitch's reachability algorithm uses O(log² n) space. What contributes to this?
options: Storing the entire adjacency matrix
Recursion depth O(log n) × O(log n) bits per frame for the midpoint node ID
A BFS queue of O(n) nodes
Dynamic programming table of O(n²) entries
questions.explanation: Each recursive call guesses a midpoint m, which takes O(log n) bits to store. The recursion depth is O(log n) because we halve the path length each time. Total: O(log n) frames × O(log n) bits = O(log² n) space.

questions.question: Why is TQBF (True Quantified Boolean Formula) PSPACE-complete?
options: It requires exponential time
The quantifier alternation (∀∃∀...) forces branching that uses polynomial space but exponential time
It is reducible to the halting problem
It can only be solved by nondeterministic machines
questions.explanation: TQBF has alternating quantifiers. Evaluating ∀x∃y∀z...φ requires exploring exponentially many branches (depth proportional to number of quantifiers) but only needs polynomial space for the current branch path. This makes it the canonical PSPACE-complete problem — the natural class for game solving and model checking.

questions.question: You need to solve a graph reachability problem in a memory-constrained embedded system. Which approach uses the least space?
options: Store the full adjacency list and do BFS — O(n) space
Use Savitch's algorithm — O(log² n) space
Enumerate all paths — O(n!) space
Store distance matrix — O(n²) space
questions.explanation: Savitch's algorithm uses O(log² n) space — exponentially less than BFS's O(n). The trade-off is time: Savitch is O(2^(log n) × poly(n)) = O(n^(log n)), which is slower than BFS's O(n + m). But when memory is the bottleneck, Savitch wins.
